name: Danish Rye Bread with Butter and Cheese

ingredients.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps: Butter the rye bread and top with the cheese.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps_detailed: Butter the rye bread generously, top with the cheese, and finish with a little black pepper.
Dense rugbrod is eaten constantly in Denmark, plain or topped with cheese, cold cuts, or fish.

name: Danish Oat Porridge
steps: Simmer the oats in the milk for 6-8 minutes, stirring, until thick.
Top with the honey.
steps_detailed: Simmer the oats in the milk and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring often, 8-10 minutes, until thick and creamy.
Top generously with the honey.

name: Danish Eggs with Herring
steps: Scramble the eggs gently.
Fold in the herring and dill just before they finish setting.
steps_detailed: Scramble the eggs gently in the butter over low heat, stirring often for soft curds.
Fold in the herring and dill just before they finish setting - fish at breakfast is entirely ordinary in Denmark.

name: Danish Smorrebrod with Herring

ingredients.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps: Top the rye bread with the herring, onion, and dill.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps_detailed: Butter the rye bread, then top generously with the herring, onion, and dill.
Smorrebrod, open-faced and eaten with a knife and fork, is a genuine art form in Denmark - a proper spread can run to dozens of topping combinations.

name: Danish Smorrebrod with Egg and Cheese

ingredients.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps: Butter the rye bread and top with the egg and cheese.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps_detailed: Butter the rye bread generously, top with the sliced egg and cheese, and finish with black pepper.

name: Danish Frikadeller (Veal Meatballs)
steps: Mix the veal with the flour, egg, and onion, and form into small oval patties.
Pan-fry in a little oil, 4-5 minutes per side, until browned and cooked through.
steps_detailed: Mix the veal with the flour, egg, onion, milk, and pepper thoroughly, and form into small oval patties.
Pan-fry in a little oil over medium heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until deeply browned and cooked through.
Frikadeller, traditionally a pork-veal blend, are the closest thing Denmark has to a national dish, served at nearly every family dinner.

name: Danish Pork Belly with Sweet-Sour Cabbage
steps: Roast the pork belly at 400F for 25-30 minutes until well browned.
Simmer the cabbage with the sugar and lime juice for 15 minutes until tender and glossy.
steps_detailed: Roast the pork belly at 400F (200C) for 28-32 minutes, until well browned and rendered.
Simmer the shredded cabbage with the sugar, lime juice, and butter over medium-low heat, 16-18 minutes, until tender and glossy.
Sweet-sour red cabbage (rodkal) is the standard partner to roast pork across Denmark - plain cabbage stands in for red here, since a distinct red cabbage entry isn't in this database.

name: Danish Flaeskesteg (Roast Pork)
steps: Season the pork with the pepper and roast at 400F for 30-35 minutes until well browned.
Roast the potatoes alongside, and steam the cabbage in the last 10 minutes.
steps_detailed: Season the pork generously with the pepper and roast at 400F (200C) for 32-38 minutes until deeply browned and the fat has rendered.
Toss the potatoes in the butter and roast alongside, and steam the cabbage in the last 10-12 minutes.
Flaeskesteg, roast pork with a crackling crust, is the traditional centerpiece of Danish Christmas dinner.

name: Danish Baked Salmon with Dill
steps: Season the salmon with the dill and dot with the butter.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes until just cooked through. Finish with the lime juice.
steps_detailed: Season the salmon with the dill and pepper and dot with the butter.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 16-20 minutes until just cooked through and flaking easily.
Finish with the lime juice - simply prepared salmon and dill is a familiar pairing across Denmark's coastal towns.

name: Danish Pork and Cabbage Stew with Caraway
steps: Brown the pork with the onion and caraway.
Add the cabbage and a splash of water, cover, and simmer 20-25 minutes until tender.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork with the onion, caraway, and pepper over medium-high heat, 10-12 minutes.
Add the cabbage and a generous splash of water, cover, and simmer 22-26 minutes until fully tender.
Caraway is used throughout Danish cooking much as it is elsewhere in Northern Europe, cutting through rich, fatty pork.

name: Danish Cheese and Potato Bake
steps: Layer the potato with the milk in a small dish and bake at 375F, covered, for 30 minutes.
Uncover, top with the cheese, and bake 10-15 more minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Layer the potato with the milk, butter, and pepper in a small baking dish, cover, and bake at 375F (190C) for 32-35 minutes until tender.
Uncover, top with the cheese, and bake 12-15 more minutes until golden and bubbling.

name: Danish Liver Pate on Rye

ingredients.ingredient: Veal, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw
Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps: Simmer the liver and onion in a little water until fully cooked, about 12-15 minutes.
Mash with the butter until smooth, and spread on the rye bread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Veal, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw
Bimbo Classic Rye Organic Bread, Classic Rye

steps_detailed: Simmer the liver and onion in a little water over medium-low heat, 14-16 minutes, until fully cooked through.
Mash thoroughly with the butter and pepper until smooth and spreadable, and spread generously on the rye bread.
Leverpostej, traditionally made with pork liver, is one of the most common toppings on a Danish lunch table - veal liver carries the same rich, iron-forward character here.

name: Danish Almond Cookies
steps: Cream the butter and sugar, mix in the flour and almonds, and shape into small rounds.
Bake at 350F for 12-15 minutes until lightly golden.
steps_detailed: Cream the butter and sugar until light, beat in a little egg, and mix in the flour and almonds until it forms a soft dough.
Shape into small rounds and bake at 350F (175C) for 13-16 minutes until lightly golden at the edges.

name: Danish Pickled Herring Plate
steps: Arrange the pickled herring with the onion and dill.
steps_detailed: Arrange the pickled herring with the onion and dill, finishing with a small squeeze of lime juice - a simple, familiar cold plate found at nearly any Danish gathering.

name: Danish Oat and Honey Bars
steps: Mix the oats with the melted butter and honey.
Press into a small pan and bake at 350F for 18-20 minutes until set.
steps_detailed: Mix the oats, chopped almonds, melted butter, and honey together thoroughly.
Press firmly into a small lined pan and bake at 350F (175C) for 20-24 minutes until golden and set.
Cool completely before cutting into bars.

name: Danish Risalamande (Rice Pudding with Almonds)
steps: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar for 25-30 minutes until thick and creamy.
Stir in the almonds and chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar and cinnamon over low heat, stirring often, 28-32 minutes, until thick and creamy.
Stir in the chopped almonds and chill at least 1 hour before serving - risalamande, with a whole almond hidden inside for luck, is Denmark's Christmas Eve dessert.

name: Danish Almond Pastry Cake
steps: Cream the butter with the almond paste, beat in the egg, then fold in the flour.
Bake at 350F for 20-22 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.
steps_detailed: Cream the butter with the almond paste and sugar until well combined, then beat in the egg.
Fold in the flour, pour into a small lined pan, and bake at 350F (175C) for 22-26 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.
Almond paste turns up throughout Danish baking, giving pastries their distinctive marzipan-like depth.

name: Danish Apple Cake (Aeblekage)

ingredients.ingredient: Apples, raw, granny smith, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

ingredients.note: toasted, standing in for cake crumbs

steps: Simmer the apple with a splash of water and half the sugar until soft, then mash lightly.
Toast the flour in the butter until golden, layer with the apple in a glass, and top with the remaining sugar.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Apples, raw, granny smith, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

ingredients_full.note: toasted, standing in for cake crumbs

steps_detailed: Simmer the apple with a splash of water, half the sugar, and the cinnamon over medium heat, 12-15 minutes, until soft, then mash lightly, leaving some texture.
Toast the flour in the butter over medium heat, 4-5 minutes, until golden and fragrant, standing in for the buttered cake crumbs used traditionally.
Layer the toasted crumbs and apple in a glass, finishing with the remaining sugar - aeblekage is assembled rather than baked, a simple layered dessert built around the apple harvest.

name: Danish Mustard Dill Sauce
steps: Whisk the mustard, dill, and honey together into a smooth sauce.
steps_detailed: Whisk the mustard, dill, honey, and oil together into a smooth, glossy sauce.
This mustard-dill combination is the classic pairing for gravlax and other cured or smoked fish across Denmark.

name: Danish Sweet-Sour Cabbage
steps: Simmer the cabbage with the sugar and lime juice and a splash of water, 15-18 minutes, until tender and glossy.
steps_detailed: Simmer the cabbage with the sugar, lime juice, butter, and a splash of water over medium-low heat, 16-20 minutes, until tender and glossy.
Rodkal, sweet-sour cabbage, is the standard side to roast pork and duck across Danish holiday tables.
